items.title: How to make a decision? Trust the wisdom of the masses
items.author: Celine M. Cammarata, Lindsey L. Glickfeld
items.content: <article>How is information in sensory cortex used to guide behavior? In this issue, Bounds and Adesnik manipulate the activity of specific neural ensembles to demonstrate that total network activation, not just activation of the most sensitive neurons, determines behavioral outcomes.</article>

items.title: Danger is coming for the 100th time: Run or stay?
items.author: Jing Cai, Dayu Lin
items.content: <article>Immediate escape and gradual habituation are both crucial for animal survival in response to repeated threat exposures. In this issue of Neuron, Liu et al. identified key neural circuits supporting each of these two responsive patterns to repeated visual threats.</article>

items.title: Deep cortical layers are more vulnerable in Alzheimer’s disease
items.author: S. Zehra H. Kazmi, Corette J. Wierenga
items.content: <article>Network hyperexcitability is one of the hallmarks of the early stage of Alzheimer’s disease (AD). In this issue of Neuron, Papanikolaou and coworkers show that AD-mediated alterations start at the excitatory-inhibitory subcircuit in the deep layers of the cortex.</article>

items.title: A p16 pathway to prevention: Senescence as a driver of tau-mediated neurodegeneration
items.author: Jeff Y.L. Lam, Kai Wang, Guojun Bu
items.content: <article>In this issue of Neuron, Graves et al. report that genetically reducing senescent cells by deleting p16 in a tauopathy mouse model significantly lessens tau pathology, neurovascular dysfunction, and behavioral deficits. Their study highlights the crucial role of p16-dependent senescence in microglia and endothelial cells as active drivers of neurodegeneration.</article>
